intro: Physics laws are universal and deterministic. Mastering fundamental equations helps you solve real-world problems involving motion, energy, forces, waves, thermodynamics, and electromagnetism. This calculator solves any variable in uniformly accelerated motion (MRUA): v=v₀+at, d=v₀t+½at², v²=v₀²+2ad. Content is updated for 2026 and reviewed regularly.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **v = v₀ + a·t  |  d = v₀t + ½at²  |  v² = v₀² + 2a·d**. Direct calculation using input values. Solve any variable in uniformly accelerated motion: final velocity, distance traveled, time elapsed, or acceleration.

explanation:
**Applied physics** solves everyday problems: from free fall to household electricity. These formulas have been validated through centuries of scientific experimentation.

## How This Calculator Works

This tool solves any variable in uniformly accelerated motion (MRUA): v=v₀+at, d=v₀t+½at², v²=v₀²+2ad. Simply enter your values and get instant results. The calculator checks inputs against typical physics ranges and warns when values seem unrealistic.

## Important Notes

These results are for reference and educational purposes. For critical decisions (especially in engineering or safety applications), consult with a qualified professional. Information is current for 2026 and updated regularly.

faq.a: Motion with constant acceleration. Objects move in a straight line with velocity changing at a constant rate, like a car uniformly accelerating on a straight road.

faq.q: How do you calculate final velocity?
faq.a: Using the formula v = v₀ + at, where v₀ is initial velocity, a is acceleration, and t is time elapsed.

faq.q: How do you find stopping distance?
faq.a: Using d = v² / (2a). If you double your speed, you quadruple your stopping distance—a critical safety fact.

faq.q: What is free fall in physics?
faq.a: Any object falling under gravity alone (9.81 m/s²) with no other forces. This includes dropped objects and objects thrown upward.
Yes. Negative acceleration represents deceleration or slowing down—the opposite direction of motion.
This calculator assumes constant acceleration. For variable acceleration, you'd need calculus—integrate the acceleration function over time.
These results use standard kinematic equations validated internationally. For critical applications or safety decisions, verify with a professional.
